note: Bodyweight exercise library with progressive variants. SVG illustrations are simple line-figures for visual reference.

variants.name: Wall pushup
variants.description: Standing pushup against a wall. Easiest entry point — start here if a knee pushup feels intimidating.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="180" y1="40" x2="180" y2="170" stroke="#7C8497" stroke-width="4"/><circle cx="80" cy="60" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="75" y="70" width="10" height="40" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="5"/><line x1="85" y1="75" x2="175" y2="55" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="85" y1="85" x2="175" y2="65" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="75" y1="108" x2="60" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="85" y1="108" x2="95" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Stand arm's length from a wall, feet flat on floor
Place palms shoulder-width on the wall at shoulder height
Keep body straight from head to heels — no sagging hips, no piked butt
Lower your chest toward the wall slowly (2 seconds down)
Push back to start, breathing out on effort
commonMistakes: Standing too close — should feel some load when leaning in
Letting hips sag forward (engage core like a plank)
Flaring elbows wide — aim for ~45° from torso

variants.name: Incline pushup (counter/table)
variants.description: Pushup with hands on a counter, table, or sturdy chair. Bridge between wall and floor.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="110" y="110" width="80" height="6" fill="#1A1F2E"/><rect x="115" y="116" width="4" height="54" fill="#1A1F2E"/><rect x="180" y="116" width="4" height="54" fill="#1A1F2E"/><circle cx="50" cy="100" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="58" y1="108" x2="100" y2="125" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="100" y1="125" x2="165" y2="155" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="105" y1="118" x2="125" y2="110" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="125" x2="130" y2="112" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Hands shoulder-width on a sturdy surface — kitchen counter is perfect height
Walk feet back until body forms a straight diagonal line
Tighten core and glutes throughout the movement
Lower chest to the surface (3-count down), push up (1-count up)
commonMistakes: Using too-low a surface before you're ready (jumps difficulty too much)
Letting head jut forward — keep neck neutral
Half-reps — chest should nearly touch the surface

variants.name: Knee pushup
variants.description: Floor pushup from the knees. About 50% of bodyweight loaded vs. a standard pushup.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="48" y1="118" x2="85" y2="145" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="85" y1="145" x2="140" y2="155" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><circle cx="145" cy="153" r="8" fill="#1A1F2E"/><line x1="45" y1="125" x2="50" y2="155" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="55" y1="130" x2="60" y2="155" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Lie face down, place palms shoulder-width, slightly below shoulders
Place knees on the floor (use a folded towel if it hurts)
Form a straight line from head through shoulders to knees
Lower chest to within fist-distance of floor, push up
Cross ankles or keep feet on floor — either works
commonMistakes: Hips sagging or piking — keep that head-to-knee line
Half-reps from the top — chest must come close to the floor
Elbows flaring wide — keep them ~45° from your sides

variants.name: Standard pushup
variants.description: Full bodyweight pushup. The classic. Foundational upper-body movement.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="115" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="43" y1="123" x2="110" y2="140" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="140" x2="180" y2="155" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="40" y1="130" x2="45" y2="155" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="135" x2="55" y2="155" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Hands shoulder-width, slightly below shoulder line
Feet together or slightly apart
Body forms a rigid plank from head to heels — squeeze glutes and brace core
Lower chest to ~fist-height above floor (3 seconds), push up (1-2 seconds)
Look at floor 6 inches in front of hands, not at the wall
commonMistakes: Sagging hips → re-engage core, squeeze glutes
Piked hips → drop them, brace stronger
Head pokes forward — keep neck neutral and ears over shoulders
Rushing the eccentric (lowering) — slow it down to build strength

variants.name: Half squat (chair-assisted)
variants.description: Quarter to half-depth squat, optionally tapping a chair on the way down.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="100" cy="40" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="92" y="54" width="16" height="40" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><rect x="88" y="94" width="24" height="30" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="5"/><line x1="90" y1="124" x2="75" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="124" x2="125" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="92" y1="60" x2="60" y2="100" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="108" y1="60" x2="140" y2="100" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Stand with feet shoulder-width, toes slightly turned out
Hinge at hips and bend knees together, lower until thighs are halfway down
Optional: have a chair behind you, tap it lightly on each rep — don't sit
Keep knees in line with toes, weight in heels and mid-foot
Chest up, gaze forward
commonMistakes: Knees caving inward — push knees out, in line with toes
Heels lifting — drive through whole foot
Sitting on the chair → just tap it, don't rest

variants.name: Bodyweight squat
variants.description: Full bodyweight squat to parallel (thighs flat) or below.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="100" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="92" y="74" width="16" height="35" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><rect x="88" y="109" width="24" height="25" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="5"/><line x1="90" y1="134" x2="60" y2="135" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="134" x2="140" y2="135" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="60" y1="135" x2="65" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="140" y1="135" x2="135" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="92" y1="80" x2="75" y2="55" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="108" y1="80" x2="125" y2="55" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Feet shoulder-width, toes turned out ~15-30°
Initiate by pushing hips back AND knees forward simultaneously
Lower until hip crease is at or below knees (full depth) — or as deep as comfortable
Drive through full foot, push the floor away to stand up
Slight arm reach forward helps balance
commonMistakes: Stopping at half-depth — go deeper if mobility allows
Rounding lower back at bottom ("butt wink") — slight forward lean is OK, rounding is not
Knees collapsing inward — actively push them out

variants.name: Deep / paused squat
variants.description: Full-depth squat with 2-3 second pause at the bottom.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="100" cy="80" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="92" y="94" width="16" height="28" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><rect x="86" y="122" width="28" height="25" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="5"/><line x1="86" y1="147" x2="55" y2="165" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="114" y1="147" x2="145" y2="165" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="55" y1="165" x2="50" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="145" y1="165" x2="150" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="92" y1="100" x2="75" y2="75" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="108" y1="100" x2="125" y2="75" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Same setup as standard squat
Lower to full ass-to-grass depth (heels stay planted)
Pause 2-3 seconds at the bottom, maintaining tightness
Drive up explosively
If you can't reach full depth with heels down, work on ankle mobility separately
commonMistakes: Bouncing out of the bottom (defeats the pause's purpose)
Heels lifting → ankle mobility limited, regress to standard
Losing brace at bottom — keep core engaged throughout

variants.name: Static lunge (split squat)
variants.description: Lunge with feet stationary — easier balance than stepping back.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="100" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="92" y="74" width="16" height="50" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><line x1="100" y1="124" x2="70" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="70" y1="160" x2="60" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="100" y1="124" x2="140" y2="150" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="140" y1="150" x2="145" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Stand in a split stance — one foot forward, one back (~2 feet apart)
Lower until both knees are at 90° — back knee close to floor
Front knee tracks over front foot (don't let it cave in)
Push through front heel to stand back up
Hold onto a wall or chair for balance if needed
commonMistakes: Front knee shoots forward past toes — shift weight back to heel
Back knee slamming the floor — control the descent
Stance too short — step further apart

variants.name: Reverse lunge
variants.description: Step backward into a lunge, return to start. Less knee strain than forward lunges.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="90" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="82" y="74" width="16" height="40" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><line x1="90" y1="114" x2="90" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="90" y1="114" x2="160" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="160" y1="160" x2="165" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="82" y1="80" x2="70" y2="110" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="98" y1="80" x2="110" y2="110" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Start standing tall, feet together
Step one leg back ~2 feet, lowering until both knees are ~90°
Step back to start, alternate or do all reps on one side
Keep torso upright — don't lean forward
Drive through the FRONT heel to return to start
commonMistakes: Stepping too short — back knee should land far behind hip
Front knee diving inward — keep it over the second toe
Crashing the back knee — control the descent (2-3s down)

variants.name: Walking lunge
variants.description: Continuous forward-stepping lunges. Higher coordination and balance demand.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="110" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="102" y="74" width="16" height="40" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><line x1="110" y1="114" x2="155" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="155" y1="160" x2="165" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="114" x2="70" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="70" y1="160" x2="60" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Step forward into a lunge, lower to 90°/90°
Drive through the front heel and step the back foot forward into the next lunge
Keep torso upright the whole time
Take a long enough step that the front knee stays over the toe
commonMistakes: Forward knee shoots past the toes — take a longer step
Torso tipping forward — engage core, stack ribcage over hips

variants.name: Knee plank
variants.description: Forearm plank from the knees. Builds core endurance with less load.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="115" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="30" y="140" width="30" height="8" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="4"/><line x1="35" y1="127" x2="45" y2="144" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/><line x1="55" y1="125" x2="125" y2="148" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><circle cx="135" cy="148" r="10" fill="#1A1F2E"/></svg>
formTips: Forearms flat on floor, elbows under shoulders, fists shoulder-width
Knees on floor (use a folded towel for cushioning)
Body straight from head to knees — no sagging
Squeeze glutes, brace core like you're about to take a punch
Hold for time (start 15-20s, build to 45s)
commonMistakes: Sagging hips — lift them, squeeze glutes
Holding breath — breathe shallow but steady
Lifting head — keep neck neutral, look at floor

variants.name: Standard plank
variants.description: Full forearm plank. The benchmark core exercise.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="30" cy="120" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="25" y="148" width="30" height="8" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="4"/><line x1="30" y1="132" x2="40" y2="152" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/><line x1="55" y1="128" x2="175" y2="152" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Forearms flat, elbows under shoulders
Toes on floor, body in a straight line from head to heels
Pull elbows toward toes (subtle squeeze) — activates core deeper
Keep glutes squeezed and breathe steadily
Build duration gradually: 20s → 30s → 45s → 60s+
commonMistakes: Hips sagging or piking — fix the line
Shoulders by ears — keep them down and back
Holding too long with bad form — stop when form breaks

variants.name: Side plank
variants.description: Plank on one forearm, body sideways. Builds obliques and lateral stability.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="40" cy="100" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="40" y1="112" x2="160" y2="165" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="40" y1="112" x2="40" y2="165" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/><line x1="40" y1="165" x2="55" y2="165" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/></svg>
formTips: Lie on one side, forearm flat, elbow under shoulder
Stack feet (or stagger top foot in front for easier balance)
Lift hips off floor, body in a straight diagonal line
Free arm extended up or hand on hip
Hold 15-30s per side, then switch
commonMistakes: Hips dropping — lift higher, squeeze obliques
Body rotating — keep top hip stacked over bottom hip
Shoulder collapsing — push the floor away with bottom forearm

variants.name: Glute bridge
variants.description: Hip lift from supine position. Best beginner posterior-chain exercise.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="170" cy="140" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="50" y1="100" x2="160" y2="140" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="100" x2="45" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="60" y1="110" x2="30" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="160" y1="140" x2="180" y2="160" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/></svg>
formTips: Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat on floor, hip-width apart
Feet close enough to butt that you can almost touch heels with fingertips
Push through heels to lift hips until body is straight from shoulders to knees
Squeeze glutes HARD at the top (pause 2 seconds)
Lower with control
commonMistakes: Pushing through toes instead of heels — keep weight in heels
Hyperextending lower back — stop when hips are level, not higher
Going through the motions — actively squeeze glutes at the top

variants.name: Single-leg glute bridge
variants.description: Glute bridge with one foot off the floor. Doubles the load on the working glute.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="170" cy="125" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="50" y1="90" x2="160" y2="125" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="90" x2="45" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="60" y1="100" x2="30" y2="60" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Setup like standard bridge
Extend one leg straight up (or hold straight out parallel to floor)
Lift hips using only the planted leg
Keep hips LEVEL — don't let the lifted side drop
Switch sides each set
commonMistakes: Hips drop on the lifted side — focus on keeping them level
Cheating with lower back — drive through the planted heel
Going too fast — slow tempo (3s up, 3s down)

variants.name: Doorway row (towel)
variants.description: Body row using a sturdy door and towel for grip. Best home-gym pull alternative.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="170" y="20" width="6" height="160" fill="#1A1F2E"/><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="50" cy="100" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="50" y1="112" x2="50" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="58" y1="95" x2="170" y2="95" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="58" y1="105" x2="170" y2="105" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Loop a sturdy towel around a door handle — handles on BOTH sides for safety
Stand close to door, grab both ends of the towel
Walk feet forward and lean back until arms are straight
Pull chest toward door, elbows back and squeeze shoulder blades together
Lower with control, body straight throughout
commonMistakes: Door not closing securely — TEST it's locked tight before loading
Jerking with hips — pull with back, not momentum
Half-reps from the top — go all the way to chest-to-door

variants.name: Dead bug
variants.description: Anti-extension core exercise. Lying on back, alternating opposite arm and leg.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="130" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="42" y="125" width="60" height="12" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="4"/><line x1="55" y1="125" x2="55" y2="50" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="95" y1="125" x2="160" y2="55" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="75" y1="137" x2="30" y2="110" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="95" y1="137" x2="100" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Lie on back, arms straight up over chest, knees bent and lifted (90° hip and knee)
Lower opposite arm and leg slowly toward floor (keeping leg straight as you lower)
KEEP lower back pressed firmly to the floor the entire time
Return to start, switch sides
Slow and controlled — 3-4 seconds per rep
commonMistakes: Lower back arching — only lower as far as you can maintain floor contact
Rushing — defeats the purpose entirely
Holding breath — exhale as you lower the limbs

variants.name: Wall sit
variants.description: Isometric squat hold against a wall. Brutal at duration.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="170" y="20" width="8" height="160" fill="#1A1F2E"/><line x1="10" y1="180" x2="190" y2="180" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="155" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="147" y="74" width="16" height="35" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><line x1="155" y1="109" x2="110" y2="109" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="12" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="110" y1="109" x2="110" y2="180" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="12" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Back flat against wall, slide down until thighs are parallel to floor
Knees over ankles (90° at knee and hip)
Feet flat, weight evenly distributed
Arms relaxed at sides or crossed over chest
Hold for time (start 20-30s, build to 60s+)
commonMistakes: Knees past toes — slide further down the wall to get feet out
Lifting heels — keep them planted
Resting hands on thighs — they help; keep them off for true difficulty

variants.name: Superman hold
variants.description: Lying face-down, lift arms and legs together. Strengthens posterior chain.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="160" x2="190" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="30" cy="140" r="10" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="38" y1="138" x2="5" y2="110" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="40" y1="140" x2="160" y2="140" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="12" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="160" y1="140" x2="195" y2="110" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Lie face-down on floor, arms extended overhead, legs straight
Simultaneously lift arms, chest, and legs off the floor
Squeeze glutes and lower back
Hold for 5-10 seconds, lower with control
Keep neck neutral — don't crank your head up
commonMistakes: Yanking the neck up — eyes look at the floor
Lifting only legs OR only arms — do both together for full effect
Holding breath — breathe normally throughout the hold

variants.name: Bird dog
variants.description: On all fours, extend opposite arm and leg. Anti-rotation core exercise.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="45" cy="95" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="55" y1="100" x2="5" y2="100" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="8" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="55" y1="105" x2="140" y2="105" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="50" y1="107" x2="50" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="140" y1="105" x2="190" y2="105" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="130" y1="107" x2="130" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: On hands and knees, hands under shoulders, knees under hips
Brace core to maintain a neutral spine — imagine a glass of water on your back
Simultaneously extend one arm forward AND the opposite leg backward
Both should be parallel to the floor — no higher
Hold 2 seconds, return to start, switch sides
commonMistakes: Spine rotating — keep hips level (the imaginary glass shouldn't tip)
Lifting limbs too high — parallel to floor is the limit
Rushing — slow, deliberate movement is the point

variants.name: Slow mountain climbers
variants.description: From plank position, alternate knees toward chest at a controlled pace.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="105" r="12" fill="#ED7A4D"/><line x1="35" y1="117" x2="35" y2="160" stroke="#ED7A4D" stroke-width="6"/><line x1="43" y1="110" x2="160" y2="160" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="14" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="160" y1="160" x2="185" y2="165" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="8"/><line x1="105" y1="135" x2="85" y2="100" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Start in a high plank (arms extended, hands under shoulders)
Pull one knee toward chest, foot off floor
Return that leg, simultaneously bring the other knee in
Keep hips LOW — don't pike up
Slow tempo: 1 second per leg
commonMistakes: Hips bouncing high — keep them level with shoulders
Going too fast and losing form — slow down
Hands too far forward — keep them under shoulders

variants.name: Step-up
variants.description: Step up onto a sturdy bench/chair, alternate legs.
variants.image: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="100" y="110" width="90" height="50" fill="#1A1F2E"/><line x1="10" y1="170" x2="190" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="70" cy="60" r="14" fill="#ED7A4D"/><rect x="62" y="74" width="16" height="40" fill="#ED7A4D" rx="8"/><line x1="70" y1="114" x2="130" y2="108" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/><line x1="70" y1="114" x2="50" y2="170" stroke="#1A1F2E" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>
formTips: Find a sturdy step, chair, or bench (knee-height ideal for beginners)
Step up with one foot, drive through that heel to bring the other up
Step back down with control, alternate or do all reps on one side
Don't push off the bottom foot — make the top leg do the work
Light hand on a wall for balance if needed
commonMistakes: Using bottom leg to push off — defeats the exercise
Stepping onto an unstable surface — your knees will thank you for choosing stable
Slamming the bottom foot down — control the descent

warmup.name: Arm circles
warmup.instructions: Standing, swing arms in big circles forward, then backward. Loosens shoulders.

warmup.name: Leg swings
warmup.instructions: Hold a wall, swing one leg forward and back. Then swing it across the body. Switch sides.

warmup.name: Hip circles
warmup.instructions: Hands on hips, big circles with hips — like hula hoop.

warmup.name: Jumping jacks (low impact)
warmup.instructions: Step out to the side instead of jumping if knees are sensitive.

warmup.name: Slow squats
warmup.instructions: Body-weight, controlled. Primes lower body for the workout.
